data.title: Oklo: Positioned To Power The AI Infrastructure Boom
data.description:
Oklo Inc.'s (OKLO) investment opportunity stems from a unique combination of technology, business model, and competitive advantages relative to other SMRs. The company has created a vertically integrated SMR with an in-house design for reactors, fuel recycling capabilities, and a behind-the-meter delivery model, which uniquely addresses one of the biggest growth areas in AI compute infrastructure today: access to reliable power.

Its unique ability to recycle nuclear fuels enables it to benefit from lower costs compared to using new uranium, potentially providing protection from volatility in the commodity's prices. Additionally, Oklo is able to deploy its reactor in close proximity to customers and avoid long wait times associated with obtaining a connection to the existing utility grid. Given the rapid expected growth rate of AI-powered computing demand, Oklo will be providing clean energy solutions to data center operators for years to come.

A second key factor driving early interest in Oklo is the size and quality of the agreements signed by the company with major hyperscaler clients, including Meta, along with large non-binding commitments from Switch and Equinix. While most of this potential capacity does not come with the certainty of a binding PPA agreement, even moderate success (deploying 1–2 GW) could result in hundreds of millions of dollars of high-margin EBITDA for Oklo due to the attractive economics of the business.

The company has also established a viable regulatory strategy, which provides a substantial timeline advantage over all other SMR companies. Oklo has chosen to leverage the DOE's Pilot Program instead of pursuing a full license via the NRC, meaning the company will be able to complete construction of its first reactor prior to receiving full commercial licensure. This allows Oklo to begin generating time-sensitive operational data and reduce the risks associated with future deployments. With recent favorable pro-nuclear policies and a well-funded near-term balance sheet consisting of Oklo's $1.4 billion in cash and marketable securities, the company appears ready to move into commercialization. With promising contracts and regulatory strength, I rate this stock a Buy.

Business Overview

Oklo operates as an entirely vertically integrated fission-technology company creating "Aurora powerhouses", which are compact (~1-meter diameter) sodium fast reactors capable of producing electric energy with outputs of up to 75 MW. The reactor designs utilize passive safety systems that will shut down without active intervention. This represents a meaningful difference from the typical design of large light water reactors and removes one of the primary sources of regulatory and operational risk.

While many small modular reactor (SMR) companies are developing similar reactor technologies, Oklo differentiates itself with its fuel strategy. The Oklo-designed "Aurora Powerhouse" may be operated using recycled spent nuclear fuel. The US alone has accumulated more than 90,000 metric tons of this "waste" over several decades of electricity generation. In the long term, if this capability were to become a reality, Oklo believes it would significantly reduce the cost associated with acquiring fuel, providing insulation from uranium price volatility that other traditional nuclear companies cannot provide.

In addition to its commercial power generation activities, Oklo has formally established two additional business units: fuel and isotopes. The fuel unit includes all aspects of its own reactors; however, it also has the potential to evolve into a third-party service provider for the advanced nuclear community. The isotopes unit is still in its early stages but is currently proceeding with the construction of the Groves Test Reactor located in Texas and anticipates criticality by July 4th of this year. High-value isotopes are used in healthcare, defense, and aerospace applications, presenting a highly-profitable ancillary market opportunity that few, if any, of Oklo's nuclear competitors are actively pursuing. This provides optionality to the company and additional upside that competitors don't have.

AI-Driven Power Demand

The growth in data center build-outs will continue to drive Oklo’s commercial opportunities, with US power demand from AI increasing by ~23% annually. Around 2,600 GW of electric generating facilities are now queued to be connected into the existing electrical grid, with a median of five years before being permitted and constructed. This delay between permitting, construction, and connecting the new generating facility into the grid is due to structural limitations within the current system.

Oklo’s behind-the-meter model is designed to address this bottleneck. With its Aurora powerhouse collocated at a data center, customers can obtain reliable, low-cost clean baseload power while bypassing the lengthy interconnection queue. Obtaining this reliable/low cost power is not only an issue of power flexibility, but it may also be the deciding factor in whether a data center can begin operations in 2028 versus 2032. This has resulted in a rapidly growing pipeline of potential customers as they recognize these benefits.

In January 2026, Meta (META) agreed to partner with the company to move forward with developing a 1.2 GW power plant located in Pike County, OH. The planned facility will provide clean energy to support Meta’s Prometheus AI supercluster located in New Albany. The agreement included a prepayment to support the purchase of fuel for the project, representing a solid level of commitment rather than just an expression of interest. Oklo expects the first phase of the facility to come online in 2030.

Prior to this announcement, Switch announced a 12 GW non-binding Master Power Agreement with the company valid through 2044. Before that, Equinix had entered into a letter of intent for 500 MW with a corresponding $25 million prepayment. All in all, the ~14 GW of potential customer commitments represent a demand signal for Oklo’s services that no other nuclear competitors currently have. Given the high-margin economics of these deals, the current valuation could appear insignificant if one or more of these deals materializes.

Regulatory Path and Milestones

Oklo's regulatory strategy is going through the Department of Energy’s (DOE) Reactor Pilot Program instead of the standard NRC licensing pathway. This is another one of the big differences between it and the rest of the SMR industry. Under the DOE’s Pilot Program, Oklo will have the ability to build and operate an Aurora reactor at Idaho National Lab (INL) prior to obtaining commercial licensure by the NRC. This allows the company to obtain operational data from a commercially constructed and operated plant while it works through the rest of its commercial licenses.

In July, Oklo completed the NRC pre-application readiness assessment and stated its intent to submit the first phase of its Combined License Application for the Aurora-INL Reactor during 2025. In November, the DOE approved the Nuclear Safety Design Agreement (NSDA) for the A3F fuel fabrication facility at INL. This was the first time such an agreement had been approved for an advanced nuclear fuel fabrication facility, and the NRC also agreed to expedite review of the company's Principal Design Criteria tropical report. These are key components needed to advance Oklo's licensing progress for its reactor design.

Besides licensing, Oklo has also benefited from regulatory tailwinds, something that should continue given the current demand for clean energy. The ADVANCE Act of 2024 was passed to help remove regulatory barriers associated with the NRC's licensing process for advanced reactors. Also in May 2025, executive orders were issued directing all federal agencies to work together to facilitate faster and easier deployment of nuclear technologies. According to Oklo's CEO, Jacob DeWitte, the regulatory landscape has gone from being a "light tailwind" to a "strong tailwind" in favor of deploying nuclear power plants. This is important to note for a company largely held up by regulatory challenges.

Oklo selected Kiewit as the lead construction contractor for the Aurora-INL project, and expects to have commercial operations online sometime between late 2027 and early 2028. Even at that time, the company will have achieved a substantial head start over almost all other developers of SMRs in the US. Once Oklo has an operating reference plant, it is likely that the licensing pathway will become much easier for future units. This puts them at the center of the clean energy race, with a differentiated solution whose potential seems to be undervalued.

Upcoming Earnings

Oklo reports its Q1 2026 results on May 12, 2026, with expectations of -$0.19 EPS. This is lower than Q4 2025 EPS, which was -$0.27, but is still worse than Q1 2025 EPS of -$0.09 YoY. Given the company's pre-commercial phase, the focus should be on project and regulatory updates rather than financials.

Oklo provided 2026 operating cash guidance of $80-100 million, which represents a ramp-up in personnel growth and project spending. Additionally, management indicated it expects to invest cash of -$350-450 million for other projects across power, fuel, and isotope units. Key project updates to look at include the status of the Aurora-INL timeline, as well as if there is any news regarding the criticality date set forth for the Groves reactor. If the Groves criticality date is moved beyond July 4th, it could cause worsening expectations.

As for funding, with an ATM raise of $1.182 billion completed in January 2026, Oklo's balance sheet provides enough liquidity to cover all near-term project expenses that should extend to other projects as the company advances toward commercialization. Since the end of 2025, shares outstanding have increased ~48% YoY from equity raises made throughout this period, allowing Oklo to invest and pursue opportunities outside its flagship technology, such as isotopes.

Valuation

The 14 GW pipeline of customers represents the maximum amount of total capacity that Oklo could potentially serve today. While the company will not likely realize all of that capacity, as most contracts are non-binding, even modest amounts may be enough. For example, 1-2 GW of operating plants producing power at a cost of $80-100 per MWh under long-term PPAs would still result in significant revenues. 1 GW of production at $90 per MWh and 85% capacity results in approximately $670M in annual revenue. Using EBITDA margins comparable to other nuclear owner/operators, which approach 60-70%, 1 GW produces approximately $400-$470M in EBITDA. If Oklo is able to get just half of its pipeline (7 GW), it equates to ~$3 billion in EBITDA, which is just under one-fourth of its current market cap. Given the long-term potential of nuclear power and strong demand, this could expand the multiple rapidly, especially if Oklo is able to win more contracts.

With the company's net-cash position of ~$1.2 billion, cash value per share is ~$7.07 with the current share count of ~174 million shares. Subtracting this number from current prices of ~$67 implies investors are currently willing to pay approximately $60 per share (or ~$10.5 billion in market cap) for the pipeline and technology related to Oklo. For a company that has received a payment from Meta Inc., signed a construction contract with Kiewit, and is building two demonstration power plants on-site at INL, the current valuation appears to be undervalued. With only modest execution needed to earn large amounts of EBITDA and a short regulatory pathway compared to peers, I believe Oklo is a Buy.

Risks to the Thesis

The main danger to Oklo is that its construction and license will take longer than they expect. The expected timeline for commercial operation (late 2027/ early 2028) is Oklo’s, not the NRC’s. There has been a long history of problems with advanced nuclear projects, including cost overruns and delayed completion; however, the company's Aurora design is smaller and less complex than traditional light water reactors. The DOE pilot program pathway is also much more flexible than the normal NRC process. With this pathway and construction already underway on the Kiewit contract, I see Oklo as having relatively less risk when compared to peers.

The second major risk is that the customer base appears stronger than it is. The Switch Agreement for 12 GW is non-binding, and phase one of the Meta deal does not begin until 2030. If AI capital expenditures are reduced or hyperscalers develop alternative methods to obtain clean power, previous LOIs may be cancelled. While the prepaid structure of both the Meta and Equinix Agreements provides some level of protection against this type of cancellation, it doesn't guarantee that customers will continue with the non-binding agreement.

The company is still pre-revenue, meaning dilution is likely as Oklo works toward commercialization. This has been partially mitigated by current cash and marketable securities numbers totaling $1.4 billion, which should last them around two years (given current guidance of $80-100 million in operating expenses and investing burn of $350-450 million). However, this could be extended over time with the use of project-level debt.

Takeaway

Oklo is an example of a potential high-risk/high-reward infrastructure investment opportunity that benefits from strong AI demand for clean energy. Currently, the company's valuation is based on projected future performance; therefore, investors are buying on the assumption that Oklo will be able to build and license reactors for customers. If the company can deliver on time and convert contracts into revenue, it has the potential to quickly evolve into a primary source of energy for AI-related infrastructure needs. Oklo also has additional optionality through other segments, such as fuel and isotopes, which could help make up losses in the event of nuclear licensing delays. Given these factors and the strong demand for clean energy, I rate this stock a Buy.

data.title: Ucore: Adroit Innovator In REE Tech But Uncertainty Remains
data.description:
Ucore Rare Metals (UURAF) is one of several North American rare earth-related micro-caps—including MP Materials (MP), Critical Metals Corp (CRML), and USA Rare Earth (USAR)—now repositioning themselves amid the growing geopolitical conflict between China and the US over the issue of REE access. Over 80,000 US defense components in 1900 weapons are now dependent on REE oxides subject to Chinese licensing controls, with entire missile pulsion and guidance systems pivoting on what element Beijing bureaucrats will allow export.

Marginal penny stocks before 2024, these firms are now emerging as key players in the West’s (very belated) effort to make from whole cloth a domestic rare earth supply chain within two years. (See prior article on this issue)

Founded in 2006, Ucore struggled for 13 years as an E&P company but has since rallied back as a "refining play." Headquartered in Halifax (and listed in the Toronto exchange—UCU:CA), Ucore initially focused on uranium and rare earth mining, but it has recently evolved into a tech innovator in the REE processing space. The firm is far less known than its peers, though it does have several attributes that could position it for later success:

  • A patent-pending separation method.
  • Funding and distribution contracts from the US Department of Defense.
  • A 10-year offtake agreement with Critical Metals Corp. to receive HREEs from its proposed Tanbreez mine, one of the world’s largest REE deposits.
  • Bokan --ownership of one of the highest-grade HREE sites in the United States.

At the center of Ucore’s viability is RapidSX, a refining technology that matches the output and quality of traditional solvent extraction but significantly improves speed while reducing environmental impact. The process was built with Western high environmental standards in mind (in contrast with China’s historically cavalier approach).

On April 7, the company released a Phase 1 report that touted its superiority compared to existing conventional CSX methods:

This Phase 1 report gives dramatic color to how well UURAF’s tech does in a head-to-head competition. RapidSX provides an extraction rate that is 5.4x faster and a settling rate that is 1.8x faster. There is better operational flexibility (i.e., being turned on and off, targeting only banned REEs, or separating each REE with several different purities), and with a far smaller footprint. It also generates a 34% reduction in CAPEX with much better ESG metrics. The numbers suggest commercial execution can be profitable.

This Phase 1 was formally accepted by the DoD and triggers a payment, as per their 2025 agreement. Last year Ucore secured $18.4 million in funding from the U.S. Army Contracting Command to support construction of its first Strategic Metals Complex in Alexandria, Louisiana, and the groundbreaking took place in May 2025.

Two weeks ago Ucore suggested that it had received and was installing a 4000-gallon leaching reactor, with the transfer of additional technology from their Ontario location. In that press release, the firm stated that it aims for full commercial commission of RapidSX in Louisiana in the first half of 2027.

Crucially, all the plant components—from pumps to piping—of this Alexandria, LA plant are sourced from North American suppliers. The facility has also received DPAS (Defense Priorities & Allocations System) status, ensuring its supply chain is prioritized for national defense needs.

Back in October, China initiated new export controls via intrusive licensing and “regulatory slow walks” on specific HREE products and REE processing-related technology more generally. The licensing actively restricts military or dual-use in order to “safeguard world peace.”

Ucore was the only REE company to quickly respond to the Beijing announcement (probably due to its tech element), stating that it “does not require or intend to use” equipment sourced from China for its U.S.-based heavy rare earth (HREE) separation operations.

In September, Ucore entered a "friend-shoring" partnership with Aussie firm Metallium to create a more streamlined refining process. Metallium will use its Flash Joule Heating (FJH) technology in Houston to convert feedstocks into high-purity chlorides, simplifying the upstream stage while Ucore’s RapidSX system in Louisiana then completes the separation into individual rare earth oxides. Thus, the two aim to build an end-to-end TX-LA refining pathway.

The CRML Partnership & Bokan

In August, Ucore and Critical Metals signed a “letter of intent” for a 10-year supply agreement. Under this deal, CRML will deliver at least 10,000 metric tons annually of rare earth concentrate from its Tanbreez project in Greenland—approximately 10% of the site’s projected output. Most of this will go into the DoD’s supply chain.

As I have discussed in my CRML report, Tanbreez is one of the world’s richest REE deposits, with an estimated 45 million tons of material, 28% of which consists of HREEs. Located near Qaqortoq in Greenland, the site also benefits from low uranium and thorium content, making it environmentally attractive. Its southern all-year access via a deep-water fjord means easy shipping. The mine will have a 25-year lifespan.

Critical Metals expects to complete its pilot plant by May and see production in 3Q 2027. Earlier projections estimate a net present value of roughly $3 billion, with a phased production plan beginning at 85,000 tons annually in 2026 and potentially reaching 425,000 tons by 2030. Ucore is expected to serve as the initial processing partner.

In addition to Tanbreez, Ucore owns the Bokan Mountain project in Alaska, home to the highest-grade HREE deposit in the US. Located on Prince of Wales Island, the site contains significant amounts of dysprosium, terbium, and yttrium—the heavy rare earths that are critical for advanced technologies such as electric vehicles and military systems.

Despite early development efforts, Bokan has remained unmined due to competition from low-cost Chinese supply. However, shifting geopolitical dynamics and rising prices may revive its viability. Earlier estimates suggest a deposit totaling 5.2 million tons, with roughly 40% consisting of heavy rare earths.

Financials & Outlook Issues

Ucore's stock has risen nearly 288% over the past year, even though the company lost US$28.6 million in 2025, far worse than the $9.4 million loss of 2024. The ticker presently has a market cap of roughly $478 million following a sharp speculative surge.

Of course, 2025 was the costly year of the RapidSX buildout -which got done and is now through phase 1—but it suggests how risky this pre-revenue company is. (The company’s quarterly financial disclosures remain unaudited—typically a concern for investors, though the yearly statements are). The company lost US$5.7 million in its 4Q 2025, far better than the $16.3 million loss, which was when the Louisiana buildout was in full swing.

At present, the company has $19 million in cash, with total assets of US$60.9 million versus total liabilities of $9.1 million ($5 of which is short-term borrowing).

Still, the rare earth landscape has changed dramatically in 2025. Ucore and its competitors are now being actively supported by the US government as part of a broader US strategy to reduce reliance on China. The company’s Louisiana facility represents its first real step toward serious production, with a commercial start planned early 2027.

According to the company website, the company will be processing feedstock by mid-2026, perhaps via the Canadian location:

This facility will be commissioned in stages, with production scaling up from 2,000 tons per annum (tps) in 2026 to 5,000 tpa in 2027 and a further potential increase to 7500 tpa in 2028.

Under favorable pricing conditions, profitability by 2028 is plausible, with the company projecting up to $750-800 million in annual revenue by 2030. The CRML off-take agreement to supply 10,000 metric tons of REE a year kicks in, in 2027 and goes for 10 years, with Ucore producing high-purity rare earth oxides from the mixed rare earth carbonates.

According to the August 2025 press release, Ucore’s CEO Pat Ryan declared:

Critical Metal Corps’s Tanbreez offers tremendous opportunities for Ucore given the significant concentration of heavy rare earths it contains, which are essential for our processing facility in Louisiana, and our downstream partners. Both Critical Metals Corp and Ucore share a vision to lessen China’s grip of the rare earth ecosystem in the West, and we look forward to our partnership, positioning us both to meet the growing demand for rare earths while addressing national security challenges.

This Ucore offtake agreement was CRML’s first buyer(!). It shows just how much operational ground has been covered in the past year that Ucore’s plant (initiated in May 2025) is now through Phase 1 and CRML is expected to have a pilot facility set to open next month. (See my Tanbreez article here).

Back in October, the U.S. government explicitly shot down reports that it was considering a stake in CRML. Now, with the clearer full ownership, it may well now invest in CRML as it did with USAR and MP.

The risks to US manufacturing from China's new regime of REE licensing are made explicit in this Ucore slide:

Risks Remain

Ultimately, the investment thesis for Ucore—and the sector as a whole—rests on a single premise: that the U.S. cannot return to dependence on Chinese rare earths and must now rebuild an entire supply chain domestically. Since the first REE embargo in 2010, geostrategic tensions have become far more explicit, and China’s REE dominance has only grown more perilous.

The big questions remain, however, for Ucore investors.

Can this tiny REE refining company find its niche in the growing Western competition --MP, USAR, and Lynas Rare Earths (LYSCF)—especially when a lot of supply comes online by 2027-2028? Yes, it has the DoD contract, but will there be robust commercial demand for all of them?

In addition, these firms will still be coping with that ever-present China price. Even as products from the mainland look to be walled off from Western manufacturers, Chinese REEs may still be periodically dumped into the West to disrupt these companies—just as what happened in 2012-2015. At present, the PRC's new April 2025/October 2025 export bans and the emerging “long-arm jurisdiction” restrict the "leaking" of resources into the West, but that could change overnight if enforcement is lax or targeted dumping appears strategically appealing to Beijing. Also, the Trump administration has appeared to turn away from constructive price floors, though there could be a "preferential critical minerals trade bloc" emerging with allies.

At the same time, Beijing's rapid "Made in China 2025" technological advancements—particularly in areas like EVs, data center AI, humanoid robotics, and aerospace—have dramatically increased its own domestic REE demand. In the coming decade, the mainland may consume most (or all) of its own strategic metals as it simply surpasses the West in both innovation and sheer scale.

Whether Ucore ultimately succeeds will depend not just on execution but on the durability of this new geopolitical reality (which I have described in great detail here).

Ucore has proven itself an adroit innovator, transforming itself into a go-to processing player. The company is worth keeping an eye on, though uncertainty remains for this micro-cap until early 2027. I would not add here, but would rather wait for a later entry point. Profitability will prove elusive until 2028 at the earliest.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Japanese Inflation Quickened In March, Complicating Bank of Japan Outlook
data.description:
Inflation stronger than expected despite government subsidies

Japan’s headline CPI inflation rose 1.5% year-on-year in March (vs. 1.3% in February, 1.4% market consensus), and core inflation excluding fresh food accelerated for the first time in five months to a 1.8% rate (vs. 1.6% in February, 1.5% market consensus). The index increased by 0.4% month-over-month on a seasonally adjusted basis, reflecting increased pressure from goods, which rose by 0.6%, while services also increased by 0.2%.

Looking at the details, prices for fresh food (-4.8%), gasoline (-5.4%), utilities (-4.8%), and education (-5.5%) dropped. Fresh food prices dropped for four consecutive months, mostly due to a high base last year driven by rice prices. The last three price drops were due to government energy subsidies and social welfare programmes. Aside from these items, increases became more widespread with household goods (2.7%), transport (2.1%), and entertainment (2.35%). Although core inflation is below 2% for a second month, excluding policy effects, inflation should stay well above 2%.

Inflation to accelerate further in coming months

We expect further inflation acceleration in the coming months. On top of rising global energy prices, the weak JPY should add more pressure on domestic prices. Producer prices and import prices increased quite sharply, too. Also, businesses usually adjust their retail prices in April, the first month of the fiscal year, which should further accelerate price gains. This year’s “shunto” negotiations resulted in wage growth above 5%, and increases for SMEs are quite firm as well. Thus, businesses are likely to pass these input cost hikes on to consumers.

We expect Tokyo CPI (to be released on May 1)to rise to 1.7% YoY in April (vs. 1.4% in March). We expect both goods and service prices to rise more quickly in April. Also, from May, both headline and core inflation are expected to climb above 2% again. This should support the BoJ's further policy normalisation process. We currently have 50 bp hikes pencilled in by the end of 2026.

BoJ watch

Markets widely expect the BoJ to stay on hold after local wires quoted comments from people familiar with the matter saying the BoJ would not hike in April. We understand that the Middle East situation is fluid and uncertain. But in our view, recent data suggest that energy shocks are having a more prolonged and much larger impact on inflation than on growth. This will be reflected in the BoJ’s upcoming quarterly outlook report next week. We expect the inflation outlook to be revised sharply upward. Meanwhile, downward revision of growth should be limited. That said, this is a non-consensus call, but we still expect the BoJ to make a data-dependent decision on the hike. If the market is right about the BoJ skipping the hike in April, then the BoJ’s communication should give a much stronger signal for a rate hike in June.

Inflation is expected to grow faster, supporting the BoJ's rate hikes in 2H26

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Politics And The Markets 04/24/26
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: Truck Tonnage Revised Higher
data.description:
The trucking tonnage for March made a near-term high with February revised higher. What is occurring here is the deportation of undocumented drivers, a shutdown of falsified IDs and certificates, and the truck leasing companies that sprung up creating overcapacity during the prior administration exiting the industry. The net effect is to make compliant companies such as J.B. Hunt (JBHT) more profitable just as real retail sales appear to trend higher.

This general economic trend creates a better climate for the trucking industry, retailers, and packaging companies like Packaging Corporation of America (PKG) which just reported decent demand. It is also positive for XPO (XPO), GXO Logistics (GXO), RXO (RXO) which provide services and transport.

Each of these suggestions has a strong management team and has remained a suggestion throughout primarily because the timing of these positive changes vs. their market psychology is always difficult to predict.

I am always in doubt about market psychology timing and sometimes need to reassess my management perspective, but if the management proves worthy, then holding is always a good idea until the market psychology finally takes hold.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: My Enthusiasm For Hilltop Holdings Has Declined After Q1 2026 (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Hilltop Holdings (HTH) released the financial results for Q1 2026 after the closing bell on April 23. The Texas-based holding company that operates PlainsCapital Bank reported net income of $37.8 million, which resulted in adjusted earnings per share of $0.64. This was $0.15 per share better than analysts’ expectations but $0.01 less than the $0.65 EPS recorded in the same quarter from last year. The current EPS total was also $0.05 less than what was earned in Q4 2025.

I previously wrote about Hilltop Holdings on September 2 of last year. A period of heavy insider buying from the previous month had caught my eye. This optimism gave me enough reason to overlook a few other issues with the company’s recent financial results and call its stock a Buy. Since that article was published, HTH has produced a total return of 10.1%, less than 1% behind the returns posted by the S&P 500.

A closer look at the company’s operations has led me to believe that the investment thesis for Hilltop Holdings is not as compelling as it was last September. The sentiments expressed by insider activity have also changed in 2026. The risks and potential rewards for owning HTH seem to have balanced out in recent months, giving me reason to downgrade the stock to a Hold.

Company Snapshot

Hilltop Holdings is labeled as a regional bank on Seeking Alpha, but the company does offer diversification beyond traditional banking services. The company reports its “family of companies” includes PlainsCapital Bank, Hilltop Securities, and Prime Lending. PlainsCapital has 54 branch locations throughout the Lone Star State. Founded in 1988, it is now the seventh-largest bank in Texas based on market share of deposits. Hilltop Securities is a full-service municipal investment bank and wealth advisory firm that celebrated its 80th birthday on April 6. It was ranked as the #2 municipal advisor in the US for the decade that ended in 2025 by LSEG. Prime Lending, a subsidiary of PlainsCapital Bank, is a national home lender that was named as the 2025 Best-In-Class Lender for Overall Satisfaction by MortgageCX.

At the end of Q1 2026, Hilltop’s balance sheet showed $15.7 billion in total assets, a decline of 0.9% from the end of 2025. The decrease was primarily due to a reduction in the company’s cash holdings, as its securities portfolio and loans held for investment both increased in the first three months of the year. Not including loans held for sale, the loan portfolio for Hilltop totaled $8.43 billion at the end of Q1, a 1.5% increase from Q4 2025.

A closer look at the loans held for investment by HTH is illustrated in the slide above. The company is heavily dependent on commercial real estate loans that comprise 43% of the total portfolio. Investor CRE has the larger share at 25% of the overall loan mix. The next largest shares of loans outstanding belong to residential mortgages at 22% and commercial and industrial loans at 18%.

Total deposits held by Hilltop declined 3.2%, quarter-over-quarter, to finish Q1 at $10.53 billion. Almost all of the decline can be attributed to a lower total of interest-bearing deposits. The change in non-interest-bearing deposits was relatively non-existent in the first three months of the year, landing at $2.83 billion, or 26.9% of all company deposits.

Hilltop Holdings returns capital to its shareholders through both dividends and share repurchases made in the open market. The company currently distributes a $0.20 quarterly dividend, with the most recent payout being made at the end of February. This year’s dividend is a $0.02 increase from the $0.18 that was paid last year. The company is currently on a streak of eight consecutive years with higher annual dividend. HTH has a forward yield of 2.13%. Hilltop also paid $47.5 million in Q1 2026 to buy back almost 1.24 million of its common stock at an average price of $38.40 per share.

Analysis of Hilltop Holdings

When the company’s results are broken out by business segment, it is clear that Hilltop’s banking operation is the largest contributor to its net income. In Q1 2026, PlainsCapital reported $47.1 million of income before taxes, 17.5% better than the same quarter from 2025. Hilltop Securities netted $14.8 million of pre-tax income, 60.3% more than Q1 2025. Unfortunately, the company’s mortgage origination segment lost $2.4 million in Q1 2026. However, that was an improvement over the $8.3 million Hilltop lost during the first three months of last year.

Because its banking operations play such a large role in Hilltop’s overall success, I will spend most of this analysis discussing its performance from the last quarter. As shown below, key performance metrics from the first three months of this year were mixed. Return on assets made a 21-basis point improvement to finish at 1.17%, while PlansCapital’s efficiency ratio rose from 54.1% at the end of 2025 to 55.5%. Net interest margin made noticeable progress, reaching 3.38%, an addition of 41 basis points in the last three months.

The graphs below that feature various aspects of CapitalPlains’ asset quality show a few concerns, although the overall picture is not especially concerning yet. The company experienced a 57 basis point spike in net charge-offs in Q4 2025 that improved by 36 basis points in Q1 2026. However, the bank’s ratio for criticized loans and non-performing assets slid higher in the last three months. The ratio for nonperforming assets moved up nine basis points in Q1 after declining for the three previous quarters. These trends will be worth watching in Q2 and Q3 of this year.

As of March 31, the book value for one share of HTH stood at $36.63, and its tangible book value landed at $31.97. Those numbers were an improvement of just 0.6% and 0.4%, respectively, from Q4 2025. The stock currently trades at a price-to-TBV ratio of 1.19, which is below the national average for a US bank, but given the nature of its overall operations and lack of profit growth, it does not seem entirely out of line.

Hilltop earned $2.65 per share in FY 2025. The four analysts that cover the company are calling for an EPS average of just $2.08 in FY 2026. Given the fact that Hilltop beat its Q1 estimates handily, that FY 2026 number may already be too conservative. Nonetheless, the company would have to top estimates by an average of 27.4% each quarter just to break even with last year. HTH's forward PE of 18, which is based on that FY 2026 estimate, is well above the national average for a regional bank.

One last piece of the puzzle for Hilltop Holdings worth noting is the recent insider activity for its stock. The optimism that was seen last year has experienced a reversal in recent months. The last six insider transactions shown on Seeking Alpha are all sells, with four different insiders involved. I do not think insider selling is always a bad sign, as there are personal financial reasons behind some transactions that would not reflect poorly on the company itself. However, this is a change from the environment discussed in my last article, when a board member had made nine purchases of 10,000 shares of HTH over a two-week span.

Additional Risks to Consider

While the diversification offered by the company’s three different business divisions can provide some advantages to shareholders during changing economic environments, Hilltop Holdings is still primarily dependent on its banking operations for a majority of its earnings. The PlainsCapital Bank operates entirely within the state of Texas, making the financial health of that state and its citizens crucial to the overall success of the company itself. The economic outlook for Texas is currently positive overall, but a few headwinds may lie ahead as changes in immigration policy have stunted job growth. Domestic migration to the state has also moderated in the last year. Much of the economic momentum in the state is being driven by AI investment and data center spending. Should that slowdown later this year, growth in Texas may become more challenging.

The company made a rather cautious statement of its own as part of its recent quarterly results. That commentary is pasted below.

The extent of the impact of uncertain economic conditions on our financial performance during the remainder of 2026 will depend in part on developments outside of our control, including, among others, changes in the political environment, the impact of tariffs and reciprocal tariffs, the timing and significance of further changes in U.S. Treasury yields and mortgage interest rates, and a volatile economic forecast. These conditions, coupled with exposure to changes in funding costs, inflationary pressures, and international armed conflicts and their impact on supply chains within our business segments during the first quarter of 2026 have had, and are expected to continue to have, an adverse impact on our operating results during the remainder of 2026.

This statement was made outside of the normal list of risks that are expected from any standard financial release and paints a somewhat foreboding tone for upcoming quarters.

The Bottom Line

Admittedly, I may have been a little late to the party for Hilltop Holdings when I first covered the company last September. HTH had gained over 16% in August, the month before my analysis was published, in large part because of the market’s interpretation of the insider activity from that month. However, the return from a purchase made on September 2 would have produced an annualized gain of almost 16% through April 24, which is still a healthy outcome.

The relatively lackluster performance of the company in the past two quarters and the prospect that earnings growth seems to have come to a halt have dimmed my enthusiasm for Hilltop Holdings. The loss of the insider tailwinds is notable as well. There are simply more compelling opportunities in the regional banking space at this time. Hence, I am downgrading HTH to a Hold.

data.title: Marvell: The AI Opportunity Is Massive
data.description:
A year ago, AI’s main bottlenecks were GPUs, TPUs, and other accelerators, which were the key spending items of hyperscalers' capital expenditure. Today this is starting to change. As computing power increases, the infrastructure that connects those items becomes the new bottleneck. We view this as a positive development for Marvell Technology (MRVL), which is likely going to greatly benefit from this due to its strategic position in the AI infrastructure industry. As such, we decided to issue a rating of Buy for Marvel.

Infrastructure Becomes a New AI Bottleneck

The market is gradually coming to an understanding that the speed of AI development is no longer limited to computing. Data transfer between chips, servers, and data centers also becomes more important. This is good news for Marvel, which develops a whole stack of solutions from optical DSP and switching to custom ASIC and silicon photonics. This allows it not just to supply individual components but to act as an integrated supplier for AI infrastructure.

Recently, the management has directly emphasized that interconnect business shows a growth rate of more than 50% per year, and this rate does not slow down. This does not appear to be a short-term surge in demand. On the contrary, it is a sign that the market is entering a new stage, where connectivity determines the effectiveness of AI systems.

This trend is also confirmed at the industry level. Investors are increasingly turning their attention to the scarcity of components for data centers, including optical solutions and memory, which only strengthens the position of players such as Marvell.

Optical and networking is the biggest hidden upside for Marvel. If you look at the structure of Marvell's business, it becomes obvious that the greatest potential is currently concentrated precisely in optical and networking solutions. These segments benefit from several factors at once. Those factors include the increasing AI load, increasing data transfer speeds, and a moving to new standards such as 1.6T and 3.2T. The company actively invests in these areas, including co-packaged optics and silicon photonics.

These are not just evolutionary improvements but, in fact, new markets with enormous potential. Some analysts already note that these segments can become the main driver of exceeding expectations in the future. Combined with a shortage of components, this creates a very favorable environment for Marvel’s growth.

Furthermore, it is worth paying attention to the company's latest M&A deal, which provides a clear indication of how Marvell plans to strengthen its position in this segment. The company recently acquired Polariton Technologies — a developer of plasmonic solutions in the field of silicon photonics, which allow for a significant increase in the data transfer speed and energy efficiency of optical connections. At first glance, it looks like a small acquisition without disclosing financial details, but strategically it has a much greater impact. Plasmon modulation allows you to scale bandwidth to levels above 3.2T, which is critical for the next generation of AI data center architectures. As AI workloads continue to grow exponentially, it is these technologies that become key to enabling data transfer between nodes. In fact, this means that Marvell is not just responding to demand, but investing in technologies that will define industry standards in the future. The combination of proprietary DSP, silicon photonics, and new plasmonics solutions creates a more integrated platform that allows the company to stay one step ahead in the optical interconnect segment. And it is this segment that we believe could become one of the most underrated growth drivers in the years ahead.

Partnership With Nvidia Is a Turning Point

Separately, it is worth highlighting the agreement with Nvidia under which the chip designer is expected to invest $2 billion in Marvell and at the same time integrate the company into its own AI ecosystem. Thanks to this, we could expect Marvell to become part of the architecture of the so-called AI factory that includes data centers of the new generation, which are built for the needs of artificial intelligence.

Within this partnership, Marvell will supply custom AI accelerators and networking solutions, while Nvidia provides compute infrastructure. Such synergy means that the company gets access to the most important market segment, which is scalable AI systems. Also, this creates a structural demand for Marvell products for years to come. The transition to the AI factory is not a one-time wave of investments but a multi-year cycle of modernization of the entire data center infrastructure, which will likely benefit Marvel for years to come.

Financial Results Confirm The Growth Story

Marvell's latest report shows that the company is not just in a winning position, but is already actively monetizing it. Marvel’s revenue for fiscal 2026 increased by 42% to more than $8 billion. This was possible thanks to the data center segment exceeding $6 billion in revenue and showing almost a 50% Y/Y growth. In addition, earnings per share during the year increased by more than 80%.

Also, the company has improved its forecast recently and now expects the revenues in fiscal 2027 to reach over $11 billion, while in 2028 it could be around $15 billion. Such a revision of the forecast over several months indicates an improvement in business visibility and acceleration of demand for Marvel’s offerings. Recently, the management has also highlighted that bookings are growing at a record pace. This means that current growth has real demand under it and not just optimistic expectations.

Another key element of Marvell's future success is the growth of the custom ASIC business. While it was almost invisible a few years ago, today it is one of the fastest growing segments of the company. The company has already reached the level of about $1.5 billion in revenue for that business and continues to actively scale to this day. This segment is now expected to continue to grow at a double-digit rate and could double in the medium term.

Marvell’s other upside is that it does not work with a single customer but with multiple Tier 1 hyperscalers. This makes it possible for the company to reduce the concentration risks. Management explicitly notes that diversification of the client base will only increase over time.

The Real Value of Marvell

Despite strong growth, Marvell has not yet received the premium that pure AI companies have. This is partly due to the fact that the market still perceives it as a traditional semiconductor player. However, Marvell’s business structure has already changed significantly. The data center segment now generates most of the revenue. If the company achieves the stated figures of $15 billion in revenue in fiscal 2028 and more than $5 EPS in the future, then its upside would be much greater than what the market currently expects.

To find out the exact upside that Marvell’s shares represent, we made a valuation model at the time when its stock was trading at $157.32 per share. Our model assumes a growth rate of 3%, which is generally consistent with our long-term assumptions for other AI names. In addition, our forecast covers the period of the next five fiscal years and is based on data from the company’s most recent earnings report, which includes cash and total debt.

The model applies a discount rate of 9.70%. When calculating the cost of debt, we primarily used the trailing twelve-month data, a 1-year unlevered beta of 1.71.

For the revenue forecast in the model, it assumes a growth trend of 37% in FY2027. We also expect an operational margin of 23% in FY2027 and a gradual increase to 39% by FY2031. The model also applies an 11% tax rate based on management estimates. Other financial assumptions remain mostly consistent with Marvell’s historical averages.

This model shows that Marvell’s enterprise value is $156.07 billion. After adding the cash balances and deducting the outstanding debt, the model showed Marvell’s equity value to be $153.92 billion, which translates to an intrinsic value of $176.98 per share. This represents an upside of approximately 12.50% at the time of this writing.

Risks

The main risk is the potential worsening of the macro environment. If hyperscalers start cutting capital costs, it will immediately affect the demand for Marvell products. Another risk is the competition in the custom silicon segment, where big players can fight for key contracts. In addition, insider sales can create short-term pressure on stocks. However, this does not change the fundamental picture.

Final Thoughts

Marvell Technology is in a unique position at the intersection of several of the most important trends that include AI, data centers, and networking. If the first wave of the AI boom was about computing, then the next one will be about infrastructure. And this is where the company has the best chance of becoming one of the main beneficiaries. Strong financial results, improved forecasting, strategic partnerships, and a large-scale market create a very convincing investment history. That is why we rate Marvell as a Buy and believe that the company remains one of the most interesting opportunities in the AI sector right now.

data.title: Kinder Morgan: Thank Storm Fern And All Of The Cold Weather After That
data.description:
Kinder Morgan (KMI) posted a fantastic first quarter because the La Nina winter provided a lot of cold weather. A midstream company like this one operates on a fee-based system. Therefore, the only way earnings go up (on existing assets being used) is for more volume to happen. In this case, an unusually cold winter storm got things going. That was followed by still more cold weather. The result was an earnings comparison that one does not often see for a company of this size.

Last Article

The last article noted when the company raised guidance for fiscal year 2025. Since weather plays such a big part in higher-than-expected volumes, this company needs to wait until later in the year to actually change guidance. As of right now, this great first quarter could easily be offset by a cool summer that uses significantly less-than-expected natural gas.

Most midstream companies in the industry will wait until the full fiscal year comes into focus before changing guidance. Since weather is a very low visibility event (and it plays such a large role in volumes handled), oftentimes that means a "last minute" guidance change (which unfortunately is subject to change as well).

First Quarter Earnings

The fact that a company this large and this diversified posted a very positive earnings comparison likely means that there will be a lot of positive first-quarter earnings comparisons. Smaller, less diversified competitors will be either "direct hits" or "direct misses" in some cases. Much will depend upon the location of the various pipelines because weather effects are not uniform.

Earnings are highly unlikely to grow at the pace shown above for the rest of the fiscal year. Furthermore, in the unlikely event that did happen, it would set up a very difficult comparison situation for all the next fiscal year. As it is now, the first quarter next year is likely to be a negative earnings comparison. As that first quarter approaches, the market will likely take that negative comparison into account.

More importantly, the company now has to wait for the important summer to determine if earnings will vary materially from the original guidance. Remember that the original guidance already had an operations growth in the guidance even though earnings per share were projected to be flat. That whole projection and continuing explanation should prove to be a forecast of what is to come when a favorable weather event like storm Fern happens to end up with a great first quarter like what is shown above.

What will help the coming comparison is the announcement of a roughly $500 million acquisition. That is likely enough to add a percentage or two to the rate of growth for the next forecast. It also helps that there are growth projects coming online.

But a company this large rarely grows at a pace to make up for the coming first quarter (negative) comparison (next year). So, while the market is likely excited about the weather, that excitement is likely muted by the steady nature of the business that "everyone" already knows.

Markets tend to value companies like this on recurring earnings from operations. What happened this first quarter was seen as an "extra bonanza" that is technically classed as nonrecurring. A similar process would happen for a warm winter or a cool summer.

Expected Operating Income Sources

This industry has a lot of fee income business that makes the business highly dependent upon volumes handled. Most businesses have downside protection in the form of "take-or-pay" clauses that provide for a minimum payment. This is where customer quality is important. Investment-grade customers are much more likely to survive a severe cyclical downturn so that the "take-or-pay" clause becomes significant. It does not become significant if the customer goes broke.

With that in mind, this company does have a secondary recovery business that is very unusual for a midstream operation. It does provide for some earnings volatility that is not seen elsewhere.

This business gives Kinder Morgan a giant head start when it comes to the carbon dioxide sequestration business. As shown above, there is already significant pipeline structure in existence. Expanding an existing business is very different from entering the business in the first place (which is what many competitors are currently "looking at").

This company knows about permanently storing carbon dioxide because that is what it does to keep the field pressure up to allow the production of oil in these fields. Furthermore, this business has been around a long time.

That positions this company way ahead of many that are reviewing this future business idea that will likely become very relevant as unconventional production wells age.

Probably the big difference is that instead of drilling for carbon dioxide as has been done in the past (and is seen in the slide above), the future will involve removing carbon dioxide from the air for the industry to use as needs increase.

As I covered elsewhere, both Occidental Petroleum (OXY) and Exxon Mobil (XOM) (and several more) are looking to increase recovery rates (as much as a triple) by using carbon dioxide in secondary recovery. If that were to actually prove to be competitive, then it could start a huge boom in this business idea. There is no assurance at this point it will happen. But it is a very visible potential area of growth at the current time.

Summary

In the long term, Kinder Morgan remains a strong buy idea for me. Now the stock price may have gotten a bit ahead of itself on the good news that came with the first quarter report. But long-term growth in the lower single digits combined with an occasional opportunistic acquisition will likely lead to an average annual return of roughly 10% to the low teens (including the dividend).

In my mind, that is not bad for a well-diversified large company in the (primarily) midstream business. But for this reason, also, I cannot argue with anyone that wants to wait out the current price for an inevitable pullback. Mr. Market tends to get a bit optimistic when good things happen and pessimistic when things "go south".

I would not sell unless the stock price gets way ahead of itself or the current growth and income story changes materially.

Risks

Everyone knows that Richard Kinder founded this company. As such, the discussion about a possible successor has been rising lately. Handling that succession when the day comes will be a big deal. Such successions are often not as successful as the original founder.

Succession planning does appear to be underway (which is a good thing). Time will tell how well it works.

The volatile part of the business is the carbon dioxide and secondary recovery business. Management does do what it can by hedging to provide certainty for the fiscal year. However, the long-term outlook for the business does depend upon the price of oil (and probably, in the future, the price of carbon dioxide).

The secondary recovery business of the company actually has a more certain long-term future outlook because it exists and it's profitable. There is a lot of competition thinking about entering the carbon dioxide business without the certainty of an existing secondary recovery business. That, of course, is much riskier.

data.title: Figma: Seat Churn Likely, Take Caution Ahead Of Earnings (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
In 2026, Anthropic reminds me of Amazon (AMZN) a few years back. At the time, Amazon was expanding into a variety of brand-new markets, such as its launch of pharmacy services in 2020. At the time, the Amazon announcement tanked pharmacy stocks like CVS (CVS). Fast forward a few years later, and Amazon Pharmacy became just another competitor. That fear that Amazon would kill the industry proved to be overblown.

That's how I think the retrospective will play out when it comes to the launch of applications like Claude Design, which immediately tanked the stocks of Adobe (ADBE) and Figma (FIG). Yes, Claude is powerful, but whether it will totally rip out every incumbent is a major question mark.

Still, Figma stock has been nothing but a sea of red this year. The stock is down ~55% since the start of the year and 85% from post-IPO highs near $115. The question now is, is Figma appropriately re-rating, or is this dip really a buying opportunity?

I last wrote a buy article on Figma in February, when the stock was trading at $24 per share. I'll admit that the timing of my buy call was poor, and the stock's ~30% crash since then warrants another look with fresh eyes. Unfortunately, I don't like what I see. I'm not buying into the Anthropic-driven panic selling, but I do see weakness in Figma stemming from other drivers: its seat-based pricing and the recent, massive push for broad-scale layoffs. Amid weak read-through from other software companies in the kickoff to the Q1 earnings season, I'm dropping my rating on Figma to neutral. To me, it's no longer an obvious buying opportunity.

Can Figma Shift Its Business Model Quickly Enough As Companies Begin Mass Layoffs?

Let's dive straight into the reason why I'm more cautious on Figma's prospects versus a few months back. Broadly speaking, I think the "SaaSpocalypse" is an overblown narrative that will prove to be as vicious toward incumbent software companies as Amazon's entry into categories like luxury, pharmacy, and pet supplies was. But I also think where AI will have the most impact on software incumbents is on layoffs. And because of this, the true impact of AI isn't a direct replacement of software products but a thinning-out of the corporate rank-and-file who use software products.

This impacts companies that price their products on a per-seat basis the most. Here is an overview of Figma's published pricing:

As shown above, Figma is hugely seat-based. Its entry-level "professional" tier starts at $16/month for a full seat, which includes access to all Figma modules (Design, Make, Draw, Sites, etc.). We do note there are consumption-based elements. You'll notice that each pricing tier also includes a certain amount of AI credits per seat, up to 4,250 credits for a full-seat user in the Enterprise tier. The upside opportunity for Figma is to upsell additional credits to its biggest power users, for whom these credits may not be sufficient enough for their monthly workloads. This opportunity is what prevents me from being overly bearish on Figma.

Meanwhile, we need to consider the impact of layoffs. Meta (META) just announced that it is planning to axe 10% of its headcount, eliminating about 8,000 jobs - which is just the latest in a long string of multiple rounds of layoffs.

Other companies have mentioned major layoffs as well. Block (XYZ), the parent company of Square and CashApp, cut 40% of its workforce in February. Square is a customer of Figma, featured prominently in Figma's case studies. Oracle (ORCL) and Snap (SNAP) are other recent examples of companies that have announced layoffs covering a double-digit percentage of their overall workforces.

Even though my overall view is that the "SaaSpocalypse" is overblown, it would be foolish to assume that these massive layoffs (which may be just the beginning of a broader corporate headline decline) won't have an adverse impact on Figma.

Q1 Look-Ahead: Expect Seat Churn To Weigh On Sentiment

As we look ahead to Figma's Q1 earnings report (expected on May 14), unfortunately early read-through from other software businesses is poor. ServiceNow (NOW) just crashed nearly 20% as growth underwhelmed, with management chalking up guidance conservatism due to the war in Iran. What investors are really looking for, however, is how protected these software businesses are from seat churn.

ServiceNow also prices many of its products on a per-seat basis, but CEO Bill McDermott disclosed on the most recent Q1 earnings call that about half of its new business revenue was seat-based, while half had graduated onto consumption-based pricing (based on cases or AI token usage). Presumably, greater than 50% of sustaining business, which has not yet been renewed, is on seat-based pricing.

I know many are interested in the progress of our hybrid business model, especially with regard to consumption pricing. You'll be happy to know that 50% of net new business now comes from a non-seat-based pricing model, including tokens and other assets such as infrastructure, hardware and connectors. Our hybrid pricing model gives customers the best of both worlds, predictable foundational seat licenses combined with usage-based scalability. It's the freedom to scale AI adoption without a friction that the customers love."

And despite evidence that ServiceNow is moving its pricing model in the right direction, the stock tanked ~20% anyway.

Figma has not yet made similar disclosures on the exact weights of its business. But back in February on the Q4 earnings call, Figma CFO Praveer Melwani noted that Figma was just beginning to transition to new business emphasizing consumption-oriented, AI credit add-ons:

Our outlook reflects the sustained strength and momentum in the business that we experienced in 2025 including benefits from our new products and offerings, seat expansion from new and existing customers, and international expansion. At this time last year, we had no customers consuming AI credits. Today, we're seeing approximately 75% of paid customers with over $10,000 in ARR consuming AI credits on a weekly basis with adoption continuing to ramp.

Our outlook reflects the seat adoption and usage patterns we're seeing today. We'll plan to refine our assumptions in the months ahead, as we continue to both learn from customer consumption behavior, and drive further AI adoption around new feature releases. This March is when our model will shift to monetizing both seats and credits, a dynamic that is not reflected in our historical revenue results."

With March being the first time that Figma is monetizing both seats and AI credits, it's safe to assume from these two sets of statements that Figma is behind ServiceNow in terms of pace in moving its subscription contracts to credits instead of seats. That's not to say that Figma won't eventually move the majority of its business to consumption (perhaps by reducing per-seat prices but lowering the amount of included AI credits to encourage users to top up more based on consumption), which will protect it from declining user counts. But in the near term, and for Q1, we are expecting:

  • Deceleration in revenue
  • A slowdown in large paid customer additions
  • Weaker dollar-based net retention rates, relative to the ~130% net expansion that Figma has been reporting

In my view, all of these factors will weigh on Figma in the near term until it can prove to the market that AI credit sales are offsetting potential loss of seats.

Valuation and Key Takeaways

I am confident that Figma will eventually drive a dominantly consumption-based model. The other factor that keeps me from being overly bearish on the stock is its decimated valuation. At ~6x forward revenue, Figma certainly isn't cheap yet, but it still sits below the likes of ServiceNow and JFrog (FROG), despite its current ~40% growth rates roughly doubling the growth pace of these peers.

That said, I do think Figma is due for a continued near-term downward re-rating as investors take revenue deceleration/seat count compression as evidence that the SaaSpocalypse is playing out against Figma's favor. For now, de-risk this position ahead of earnings and wait for further clarity on the company's consumption-based business model before diving back in.

data.title: Grab Holdings: The GoTo Merger Scenario Upside
data.description:
Bloomberg's 2022-2024 coverage of Grab Holdings Ltd. (GRAB) reflected the sentiment of most investors, frequently using terms like "money-losing" and "path to profitability". After an amazing fiscal year 2025, that view no longer holds. Revenue climbed 20%, surpassing $3.3 billion, and adjusted EBITDA increased 60% YoY to $500 million. Adjusted free cash flow came in at $290 million, almost double YoY.

Despite this financial milestone, the stock still trades around $4. In our view, the market seems to be overlooking substantial upside. Sentiment has been suppressed by the lingering potential of a merger between Grab and its largest competitor, GoTo. Regulatory concerns endure, but Indonesia’s new sovereign wealth fund, Danantara, is shifting the outlook. If approved, Grab would capture about 80% of Southeast Asia’s ride-hail and food delivery markets. Even without the merger, Grab’s management is targeting $1.5 billion in EBITDA by 2028.

Business Overview

Grab runs the largest “super-app” across eight Southeast Asian countries, offering ride-hailing, food and grocery delivery, and financial and banking services. It reports results in three categories: On-Demand Services (from ride-hailing and delivery), Financial Services (GXS digital bank in Singapore and GX Bank in Malaysia), and other new initiatives. On-Demand Services are the largest revenue segment, while Financial Services is expected to boost future growth.

The peer group is narrower than it seems. Sea Limited (NYSE:SE) overlaps via Shopee logistics and SeaMoney but lacks ride-hailing. Uber (NYSE:UBER) runs a similar model, but in different markets with different economics. GoTo and foodpanda, smaller local firms, are less profitable. Grab is Southeast Asia’s only scaled super-app combining profitability with a strong balance sheet.

Financials and Balance Sheet

While Q4 revenue rose 19% to $906 million, it’s worth noting that the company missed the Bloomberg consensus revenue estimate of $940.6 million by $34.6 million. However, most of the gap came from softer delivery take rates rather than lower volumes. On the EBITDA line, the quarter printed $148 million, beating consensus and up 54% year-over-year, and for the full year, Grab did something it had not done once as a public company: it booked a net profit of $200 million.

For 2026, management gave guidance of $4.04 to $4.10 billion in revenue and $700 to $720 million in adjusted EBITDA, implying 20% to 22% and 40% to 44% growth, respectively. On that guidance, the EBITDA margin rises to roughly 17.3% at the midpoint compared to 14.8% in 2025. The next major earnings call is Q1 2026 on May 4, 2026, with a Street consensus Q1 revenue of approximately $907.6 million. After the softer take rates in Q4, Deliveries revenue is the line investors will be looking at, and Grab is primed to deliver on it.

Management has targeted a compounded annual growth rate of 20% for revenue across the 2025-2028 plan, with adjusted EBITDA expected to triple to $1.5 billion by 2028 and free cash flow conversion climbing toward 80%. If those run rates are reached in 2028, adjusted free cash flow would exceed $1.2 billion on a current market capitalization of roughly $17 billion. At the 2025 year-end, Grab reported gross cash liquidity of $7.4 billion across cash, equivalents, and short-term investments, with net cash of $5.4 billion.

That would enable Grab to fully cover the contemplated $2 billion GoTo bridge loan, the $600 million foodpanda Taiwan purchase, and the newly authorized $500 million share buyback, with cash left over. Grab’s inaugural $500 million repurchase program was authorized in February 2024. Cumulatively through March 31, 2025, Grab had repurchased roughly $226 million in shares under that prior program, with $274 million remaining. Through Q2 2025, the company had cumulatively repurchased 126 million shares for $499.6 million, essentially finishing the first program. The newly authorized $500 million program, announced alongside the Q4 2025 print, is therefore the second tranche.

For FY2025, adjusted free cash flow was $290 million, almost twice 2024’s $162 million. GAAP operating cash flow came in at $79 million.

Valuation and Peer Comparison

Grab trades at a discount to slower-growth peers with more mature profit pools in more competitive markets. Uber’s multiple applied to guided 2026E EBITDA suggests the stock could trade over $6, which is consistent with Seeking Alpha’s Analysts’ Rating average target of $6.35.

Company Ticker EV/EBITDA (FY26E) Revenue Growth
Grab Holdings GRAB ~16.8x 20-22%
Uber UBER ~21x 14-16%
Sea Limited SE ~19x 17-19%
DoorDash DASH ~28x 18-20%

Grab’s SA Quant Rating is currently a HOLD (2.96), with grades of A+ (growth), B+ (profitability), D+ (valuation), D (momentum), and C+ (revisions). That profile points to strong growth, good margins, but weak momentum. We believe the Quant Rating is overly conservative on the valuation and momentum, especially when considering the upside potential of a merger with GoTo. Another positive signal is that the company authorized a $500 million stock buyback program alongside the Q4 earnings release, on top of the prior $500 million program that had repurchased $226 million cumulatively through Q1 2025 and was essentially exhausted by Q2 2025.

The Grab-GoTo Merger

Grab-GoTo deals have been reported multiple times since Q1 2025. Bloomberg reported that talks were still continuing in February, but Grab announced a pause of the deal in June before Danantara re-entered talks in late 2025 and early 2026. Jakarta Globe and DealStreetAsia have reported that these discussions are continuing, with Danantara CEO Rosan Roeslani and State Secretary Prasetyo Hadi confirming that they are speaking with President Prabowo’s office.

Danantara matters because if Grab merged with GoTo under the normal antitrust framework, the KPPU, Indonesia’s antitrust regulator, would likely block the deal on the basis of the roughly 80% combined market share in ride-hailing and food delivery. However, if Danantara were to acquire a minority stake and a “golden share” over driver welfare and local operations, the combined entity would then read as a domestic champion. That is something KPPU and the Prabowo administration can accept. A full foreign takeover of Indonesia’s top technology company was probably not going to be approved. Any deal with Indonesia’s sovereign fund involved is a different transaction. In the reported structure, GoTo sells its Singapore business to Grab. GoTo sells most of its Indonesian businesses to Grab. GoTo Financial likely stays publicly traded, and the bridge financing is being arranged by Grab, which held a net cash balance of ~$5.4 billion entering the talks.

With GoTo having generated roughly $1.0 billion in 2024 revenue and having crossed to positive adjusted EBITDA, the 2028 target is likely conservative, especially with anticipated efficiencies from rider supply, courier routing, and cloud operations.

The merger would not leave Indonesia without competition. GoTo is the largest domestic rival, but it is not the only one. In ride-hailing, Malaysia-based Maxim and Russia-founded inDrive have scaled fleets across Indonesian cities, particularly in tier-two geographies where they compete on price. In food delivery, ShopeeFood has become the region’s second-largest player with roughly 14% Southeast Asia share and more than 50% GMV growth in 2024, and it announced expansion into 18 new Indonesian cities in July 2025. A combined Grab-GoTo would hold a dominant position, and end the direct competition between Grab and GoTo. Although a well-funded group of smaller players would still remain in the market.

The Foodpanda Taiwan Bolt-On

Another example, not fully factored in, is Grab’s acquisition of foodpanda Taiwan for $600 million in cash. Taiwan presents a new high-income market for Grab. The target generates an estimated $1.8 billion in annual GMV across 21 cities. The acquisition is expected to close in H2 2026, subject to regulatory approval. Revenue contribution will be immediate on closing, with EBITDA accretion by 2028, and management has framed the transaction as accretive to the 2026 guide and contributing at least $60 million in incremental adjusted EBITDA by 2028. It will also be Grab’s entry into a high-income Asian market outside of Southeast Asia and independent of China-dominated geographies, diversifying the portfolio.

The regulatory path, however, is not frictionless. In December 2024, Taiwan’s Fair Trade Commission blocked Uber Eats’ proposed $950 million acquisition of foodpanda Taiwan on competition grounds, referencing concerns that the combined entity would have held roughly 90% of Taiwan’s food delivery market. Uber ultimately terminated that deal in March 2025 and paid a termination fee of approximately $250 million. Grab’s proposed acquisition is a different fact pattern: Uber Eats remains an independent operator in Taiwan, and management estimates the post-deal share of the combined Grab-foodpanda entity at just over 50%, designating it as a stronger competitor to Uber Eats rather than creating a near-monopoly. That said, the Taiwan FTC has confirmed it is reviewing Grab’s filing and has raised a specific concern: Uber holds roughly 13% of Grab’s outstanding shares, and Taiwan lawmakers have flagged the possibility that the deal could serve as a backdoor to the consolidation the TFTC previously rejected. Grab has publicly clarified that Uber’s voting stake is under 4% and that Uber does not participate in Grab’s day-to-day operations. The deal remains pending regulatory approval, and the timeline could slip if the TFTC demands structural remedies.

In short, the Taiwan bolt-on has real strategic rationale and a cleaner political runway than the GoTo merger, but it is not a rubber stamp.

Other Catalysts

Our eyes will be peeled for several catalysts likely to play out by the end of the calendar year. First, the Q1 2026 earnings report on May 4th, 2026. Should Grab deliver an earnings beat, we expect meaningful price action and bullish investor momentum. Second, a formal announcement from Grab or GoTo on the merger, or how it will potentially be structured. We are expecting to see something for the second half of the calendar year. Third, we believe any investor needs to be aware of KPPU’s position. Any positive ruling would likely cause a jump in the stock price. Fourth, we await news of the foodpanda acquisition closing by the end of the year, which will be a tangible sign of Grab’s M&A execution capability. The Taiwan FTC’s formal review outcome is the gating event there.

Risks

When dealing with M&A and tech, there are a lot of variables. Grab is not unique in this way, and there are several risks that investors need to be aware of.

The GoTo merger has intrinsic risks. Talks have been paused multiple times, most recently in June and September 2025, before Danantara revived discussions. The merger will not be completed if KPPU rejects it and Danantara is unable to offer a workable structure. In that case, Grab would still be a growing, profitable platform, with the stock missing out on a considerable part of the potential merger upside.

Political risk continues to be significant. Prabowo has been president for less than two years, and Danantara lacks a long history of making large investments in foreign-listed companies. Changes to policies governing the merger or Danantara’s mandate could interfere with the regulatory scenario. As such, investing in Grab based on the merger thesis is contingent on Indonesian policy stability.

Taiwan's regulatory risk is another potential hurdle. The TFTC blocked Uber’s prior bid for foodpanda Taiwan in December 2024 over competition concerns, and it is now reviewing Grab’s filing. The Uber shareholding in Grab creates an added angle for regulators to probe. A forced restructuring, a material remedy package, or an outright block would reduce the strategic value of the transaction and could force a termination fee disclosure.

Currency exposure is also something worth considering. While Grab reports earnings in USD, it operates in local currencies such as the rupiah, ringgit, baht, peso, and dong. A strong dollar reduces reported results despite the underlying growth in the business. Management hedges occasionally but has not yet implemented a long-term, scalable FX program.

Insider transactions are another consideration. Throughout 2025 and early 2026, there were several large sales executed by the CEO and other insiders. Generally, this is not an immediate red flag, especially when done through 10b5-1 plans. Though insider selling is still on our radar, it can be considered a lagging indicator.

Conclusion

Grab reached its first full-year net profit in FY2025. The 2026 momentum points to continued margin expansion, and management is targeting $1.5 billion in EBITDA by 2028.

Our base case assumes a 19x multiple on the 2026 guidance EBITDA midpoint, implying a share value of roughly $4.70 per share, excluding any contribution from the GoTo merger.

In contrast, our upside case layers in the GoTo merger and assumes a multiple expansion toward Uber’s 21x, applied to the higher combined EBITDA base. That math yields a valuation range of around $6.35 to $7.00 per-share, consistent with SA Analyst's Ratings, and represents potential upside of roughly 57% to 73% from current prices.

At Avalon Capital Research, we rate Grab Holdings Limited a BUY.
